coredata.dc:title: Non-enzymatic acetylcholine electrochemical biosensor based on flower-like NiAl layered double hydroxides decorated with carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lve
Chen, Xu
Liu, Changxia
Yang, Wensheng
coredata.dc:description: A non-enzymatic acetylcholine (ACh) electrochemical biosensor was constructed based on flower-like NiAl layered double hydroxides (NiAl-LDHs) decorated with carbon dots (CDs). The flower-like NiAl-LDHs with a cross-linking nanosheet structure and high specific surface area were hydrothermally fabricated. Then CDs with negative surface charge were decorated on the NiAl-LDHs with positively charged brucite-like layers to obtain NiAl-LDH/CD composites. Compared with the NiAl-LDHs, the NiAl-LDH/CD composites exhibited enhanced electroconductivity and electrocatalytic performance for ACh oxidation. The NiAl-LDH/CD composites modified glassy carbon electrode had a wide linear response range of 5–6885μM, high sensitivity of 133.20±0.03mAM−1 cm−2 and low limit of detection of 1.7μM, which are superior among the non-enzymatic ACh electrochemical biosensors. The biosensor also exhibited a good durability and long-term stability, and an excellent selectivity for ACh detection. The good performance in the non-enzymatic detection of ACh could be attributed to the synergistic effect between NiAl-LDHs and CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-enzymatic biosensor
Electrocatalysis
NiAl layered double hydroxide
Carbon dots
Acetylcholine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE
Characterization of NiAl-LDHs and NiAl-LDH/CD composites
Electrochemical behavior of NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Basic Research Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Doctoral Program Foundation of Institutions of Higher Education of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the National Basic Research Program of China (Grant No. 2014CB932103 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51272020 , 21236003 , U1407118 ), the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (Grant No. 20120010110013 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. YS1406 ). Lve Wang received her B.Sc. degree in Bioengineering from the Hebei University of Science and Technology, Hebei, PR China, in 2011. She is currently a Ph.D. student under the supervision of Prof. W. S. Yang at the Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, PR China. Her research interests are in electrochemical biosensors based on carbon dots. Xu Chen received a B.Sc. degree in 1996 and M.Sc. degree in 1999 from Jilin University, and her Ph.D. from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, PR China, in 2003. She was a postdoctoral research fellow in the Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, from 2003 to 2005. She is presently a professor in the College of Science, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. Her research interests are in nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensors and biosensors. Changxia Liu graduated from the Department of Biology at the Shandong Normal University in 1991, with the degree of BSc. She was awarded a MSc degree from the Crop Genetic and Breeding Department of the China Agricultural University in 1999, and a Ph.D. (D. E.) from the College of Life Science and Technology, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. Her research interests are in biochemical engineering and the preparation and application of nanomaterials in biotechnology. Wensheng Yang graduated from the Department of Chemistry at the Shandong University in 1994, with the degree of M.Sc. In 1997, he was awarded a Ph.D. degree from the Department of Physical Chemistry, University of Science and Technology in Beijing. He is presently a professor in the College of Science, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. His research interests are in supramolecular assembly chemistry, new energy storage and conversion materials, and electrochemical sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern and (B) SEM image of NiAl-LDHs.
(A) SEM, (B) magnified SEM, (C) HRTEM, and (D) magnified HRTEM images of the NiAl-LDH/CD composites.
FT-IR spectra of the CDs and NiAl-LDH/CD composites.
CVs of the NiAl-LDH/GCE (line a) and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE (line b) from 0.20 to 0.75V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in 0.1M KOH solution. Scan rate: 0.1Vs−1.
CVs for (A) NiAl-LDH/GCE and (B) NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE in the absence (line a) and presence (line b) of 2.0mM ACh from 0.20 to 0.75V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in 0.1M KOH. Scan rate: 0.1Vs−1.

(A) Current-time plot of the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE during the successive addition of ACh into continuously stirred 0.1M KOH solution. a=5μM, b=20μM, c=200μM, d=2000μM of ACh. (B) Corresponding calibration plot for ACh detection. Applied potential: +0.65V (vs. Ag/AgCl).
Effect of 20μM DA, 250μM AA and 2μM NE relative to 50μM ACh for NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE, respectively.
Efficiencies of various non-enzymatic acetylcholine biosensors.

head.ce:title: Non-enzymatic acetylcholine electrochemical biosensor based on flower-like NiAl layered double hydroxides decorated with carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science and Technology, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Flower-like NiAl layered double hydroxides decorated with carbon dots are synthesized, which exhibit a wide linear response range, high sensitivity, low limit of detection, good durability and long-term stability, and excellent selectivity for ACh detection.

A non-enzymatic acetylcholine (ACh) electrochemical biosensor was constructed based on flower-like NiAl layered double hydroxides (NiAl-LDHs) decorated with carbon dots (CDs). The flower-like NiAl-LDHs with a cross-linking nanosheet structure and high specific surface area were hydrothermally fabricated. Then CDs with negative surface charge were decorated on the NiAl-LDHs with positively charged brucite-like layers to obtain NiAl-LDH/CD composites. Compared with the NiAl-LDHs, the NiAl-LDH/CD composites exhibited enhanced electroconductivity and electrocatalytic performance for ACh oxidation. The NiAl-LDH/CD composites modified glassy carbon electrode had a wide linear response range of 5–6885μM, high sensitivity of 133.20±0.03mAM−1 cm−2 and low limit of detection of 1.7μM, which are superior among the non-enzymatic ACh electrochemical biosensors. The biosensor also exhibited a good durability and long-term stability, and an excellent selectivity for ACh detection. The good performance in the non-enzymatic detection of ACh could be attributed to the synergistic effect between NiAl-LDHs and CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetylcholine (ACh) is a widely studied neurotransmitter, and is involved in neurotransmission for the central and peripheral nervous systems [1]. ACh is important in many biological processes, such as learning, memory, emotions, and movement [2,3]. Abnormal levels of ACh are associated with nerve disorders including Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, progressive dementia, Schizophrenia, motor dysfunction, and so on [4]. Detecting ACh in biological samples is therefore important in neuronal cholinergic system research.

Traditional methods for ACh detection include high-performance liquid chromatography [5], colorimetric detection [6], optical detection [3,7], chemiluminescence [8,9], and photoelectrochemistry [10]. These methods are complicated, time-consuming and require expensive equipment that is not practical for widespread application. Electrochemical methods have also been used owing to their good selectivity, sensitivity, and convenience [2,11,12]. These include electrochemical enzyme-based and non-enzymatic ACh biosensors [1,2]. Compared with electrochemical enzyme-based ACh biosensors, electrochemical non-enzymatic ACh biosensors can allow for simple preparation and storage, afford good stability against denaturation, and provide effective enzyme-like catalysis over a wide pH and temperature range [6,13,14]. However, these advantages depend on the electrochemical properties of the electrode materials.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are anionic lamellar compounds composed of positively charged brucite-like layers and interlayer anions [15,16]. LDHs have a permanent layer positive charge, large surface area, good adsorption ability and anion exchange property [17–19]. Thus, they have been widely investigated for use in non-enzymatic electrochemical biosensors [20,21]. Compared with traditional two-dimensional sheet LDHs, three-dimensional flower-like LDHs are more promising in biosensors. This is because their cross-linking nanosheet structure and high surface area-to-volume ratio effectively increases the electrode–electrolyte contact area leading to fast reaction kinetics [22–25]. However, the poor electroconductivity and biocompatibility, and lack of specific recognition ability of LDHs constrain their application in biosensors [2,21,26]. To overcome these problems, carbon nanomaterials such as graphene [27,28], carbon nanotubes [21], and ordered mesoporous carbon [26] have been combined with LDHs, and the resulting composites have been used to fabricate biosensors with improved sensing performance, because these carbon nanomaterials exhibit large specific surface areas and good electrical conductivity. Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials. In addition to the above advantages of carbon nanomaterials, CDs can be functionalized with abundant carboxylic and/or hydroxyl groups, and exhibit good biocompatibility. These benefits can be combined with those of LDHs to allow for the recognition of substrate molecules. LDH/CD composites have been investigated for the removal of organic dyes [29], as electrocatalysts for water oxidation [30], in enzymatic immobilization [12], and in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 [31]. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, LDH/CD composites have not been used in non-enzymatic electrochemical biosensors.

Herein, the cross-linking nanosheet structure and high specific surface area of flower-like NiAl-LDH is combined with the good electroconductivity and biocompatibility of CDs. We have constructed a non-enzymatic ACh biosensor based on flower-like NiAl-LDHs decorated with CDs. The biosensing performances of a NiAl-LDH-modified glassy carbon electrode (NiAl-LDH/GCE) and a NiAl-LDH/CD composite-modified glassy carbon electrode (NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE) are investigated, and the role of the CDs is evaluated.

Wood-based activated carbon (AC) powder (200mesh) was purchased from Chengde Xingyuan Activated Carbon Co., Ltd. (China). 3- and 30kDa molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) membranes (Amicon Ultra-15, Millipore) were obtained from Beijing Lablead Trade Co., Ltd. (China). Acetylcholine chloride was obtained from Aladdin Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine hydrochloride was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). l-ascorbic acid was procured from Shan Tou Xilong Chemical Co. (Shantou, China). Norepinephrine was obtained from Matrix, Inc. (United States). Other chemicals were analytical grade and were used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.0, 0.1M) was prepared using 0.1M Na2HPO4 and 0.1M NaH2PO4.

Electrochemical experiments were performed using a CHI 660B electrochemical workstation (CHI Instruments, China). A three-electrode configuration was employed, with a modified glassy carbon (diameter, 3mm) working electrode, and Ag/AgCl (in saturated KCl solution) and platinum wire serving as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/MAX 2500 VB2+/PC) with Cu Kα radiation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss Supra 55, Carl Zeiss Co., Germany), at an operating voltage of 20kV and current of 10μA. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected using an electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100F 200KV). Infrared (IR) spectra of dried CDs and NiAl-LDH/CD composites were obtained from KBr pressed disks using a Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Bruker Vertex 70V).

NiAl-LDHs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment. Typically, a solution containing Ni2+ and Al3+ was prepared by dissolving Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Al(NO3)3·9H2O in deionized water. The total concentration of Ni2+ and Al3+ was kept at 0.020M, with a Ni2+/Al3+ molar ratio of 3:1. Solutions of hexamine (0.025M) and ammonium fluoride (0.020M) were then added to the mixed salt solution under vigorous stirring. The resulting slurry was treated with N2 for 30min. The slurry was then reacted in a hydrothermal reactor at 100°C for 12h. The resulting NiAl-LDH was collected by centrifugation, thoroughly washed with water and then anhydrous ethanol, and then dried at 60°C overnight in a vacuum oven.

CDs were prepared according to our previously reported method [32]. A mixture of AC and KOH (6g, mass ratio of 1:2) and stainless-steel balls (9 of diameter 20mm, 20 of diameter 10mm, and 50 of diameter 6mm) were placed in a stainless-steel vessel with a capacity of 0.1L. The balls were rotated at 500rpm for 50h using a high-energy ball mill (Nanjing University Instrument Plant, QM-3SP2, PR China). An aliquot (0.6g) of this mixture was added to deionized water (30mL), and a uniform suspension was formed by mild ultrasonication for 10min. The suspension was neutralized with nitric acid, and subsequently centrifuged at 9000g for 10min to remove non-fluorescent deposits. The supernatant was separated using a centrifugal filter device containing a membrane with a MWCO of 30kDa. The filtrate was then separated using a centrifugal filter device with a MWCO membrane of 3kDa to obtain components with a MW of 3–30kDa. The CDs of 3–30kDa were added to deionized water (6×10mL) to remove inorganic salts and low-molecular-weight organic fluorophores, thus yielding the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to use, bare GCEs were carefully polished with alumina powder (1.00, 0.30, and 0.05μm, in that order) using a wet polishing cloth, then cleaned successively with ethanol and deionized water in an ultrasonic bath, and finally dried in nitrogen.

The NiAl-LDH/GCE was prepared as follows. A 2mgmL−1 NiAl-LDH suspension was prepared by ultrasonically dispersing 2mg of NiAl-LDH in 1mL of deionized water. A total of 6μL of this suspension was deposited on the surface of a polished GCE, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate at ambient temperature. To ensure electrode stability, the obtained electrode was coated with 2μL of 0.5wt.% chitosan, and then allowed to dry at ambient temperature. The resulting NiAl-LDH/GCE was stored at 4°C until use.

The NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE was prepared as follows. A NiAl-LDH suspension was prepared by ultrasonically dispersing 4mg of NiAl-LDH in 1mL of deionized water. Next, 1mL of NiAl-LDH suspension was then mixed with 1mL of 0.1mgmL−1 CDs, and the mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 10min. This yielded a NiAl-LDH/CD composite suspension with a CD/LDH mass ratio of 0.025. Afterwards, 6μL of this suspension was deposited on the surface of a polished GCE, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate at ambient temperature. To ensure electrode stability, the obtained electrode was coated with 2μL of 0.5wt.% chitosan, and then allowed to dry at ambient temperature. The resulting NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE was stored at 4°C until use. NiAl-LDH/CD/GCEs with other CD/LDH mass ratios were prepared similarly.

Before any measurements were carried out, the prepared electrodes were transferred to the base electrolyte (0.1M KOH), and subjected to 100 cyclic voltammetry (CV) cycles across the potential range 0.20–0.75V (vs. Ag/AgCl), at a scan rate of 0.05Vs−1. This process activated the electrodes. CV measurements were then recorded from 0.20 to 0.75V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. Next, 2.0mM of ACh was added, and the current was recorded. Current–time plots for the detection of ACh by the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE were conducted at +0.65V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in 5mL of stirred alkaline solution at a rotation rate of 300rpm. The current was measured and used for the quantification of ACh.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NiAl-LDHs and NiAl-LDH/CD composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A shows the XRD pattern of the prepared NiAl-LDHs. The sample possessed a well-crystallized LDH structure, with strong and sharp characteristic diffraction peaks indexed to the (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110) and (113) planes. No extra characteristic peaks were detected, indicating no impurities were present. The NiAl-LDH particles existed well-dispersed flower-like spheres of size 1–2μm, as shown by the SEM image in Fig. 1B. The nanosheets of the NiAl-LDHs were very thin, and possessed a cross-linking nanosheet structure.

Fig. 2 A and B shows SEM images of the NiAl-LDH/CD composites under different magnifications. The NiAl-LDH/CD composites exhibited a similar morphology to that of NiAl-LDHs shown in Fig. 1B. The high-magnification SEM image in Fig. 2B shows that the NiAl-LDH/CD composites have a flower-like spherical structure with cross-linking nanosheets. This indicated that incorporating the CDs did not perturb the morphology of the NiAl-LDHs. Fig. 2C and D shows HRTEM images of the NiAl-LDH/CD composite under different magnifications. Fig. 2D shows that CDs are evenly dispersed on the surface of LDH nanosheets, without any aggregation.

We then investigated the interaction between the CDs and NiAl-LDHs. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs is shown in Fig. 3 . Peaks at 1580 and 1380cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COOH groups, respectively, and that at 3407cm−1 was attributed to the OH stretching vibration [32]. The FT-IR spectrum of the NiAl-LDH/CD composites is also shown in Fig. 3. The peaks of the COOH asymmetric stretching vibration and OH stretching vibration were shifted to 1605 and 3497cm−1, respectively. This indicated that the CDs not only physically adsorbed to NiAl-LDHs, but also had additional strong interaction with NiAl-LDHs, which came from the strong electrostatic interaction between CDs with negative surface charge and LDH nanosheets with positive surface charge, or the formation of a CONi species that is chemically bound [30]. The strong interaction between NiAl-LDHs and CDs could stabilize the NiAl-LDH/CD composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows typical CVs of the NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE in 0.1M KOH solution, in the potential range of 0.20–0.75V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at 0.1Vs−1. A pair of well-defined redox peaks was observed for the NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE. These were assigned to the Ni(II)/Ni(III) redox couple, which formed in the alkaline medium [2,26]. The peak currents for oxidation and reduction of the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE increased by nearly the same amount, compared with those of the NiAl-LDH/GCE. The oxidation peak current increased by 2.5 times upon incorporating the CDs, which suggested that the CDs improved the electroconductivity of the modified electrode.

The CD/LDH mass ratio of the NiAl-LDH/CD composites was optimized (figure is not shown). The oxidation peak current of ACh increased as the CD/LDH mass ratio increased from 0 to 0.025. This was because the CDs adsorbed on the surface of the flower-like NiAl-LDHs, which improved their electroconductivity. Increasing the CD/LDH mass ratio from 0.025 to 0.050 resulted in a decrease in the oxidation peak current of ACh. This was attributed to excess CDs increasing the water solubility of the NiAl-LDH/CD composites, which led to a decrease of composite loading amount on GCE and therefore decreased oxidation peak current. The oxidation peak current reached a maximum at a CD/LDH mass ratio of 0.025. Thus, this mass ratio was used in subsequent experiments to achieve the highest response.

Fig. 5 shows CVs recorded before and after the addition of 2.0mM ACh for the NiAl-LDH/GCE (Fig. 5A) and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE (Fig. 5B) in 0.1M KOH solution. For NiAl-LDH/GCE (Fig. 5A), upon adding ACh, the oxidation peak current increased and the reduction peak current decreased, and the increment of oxidation peak current was twice of the decrement of reduction peak current. This is in accordance with the typical CV of ACh oxidation on the Ni-based electrode in alkaline solution [20,26]. Therefore, the mechanism of ACh oxidation can be suggested as follows.

First, the oxidation of Ni(OH)2 takes place as the following reaction:
(1)
Ni
(
OH
)
2
+
OH −
→ NiOOH +
H 2
O +
e −

And then the oxidation of ACh takes place via the following reaction:
(2)
NiOOH + ACh → Ni
(
OH
)
2
+ product
Reaction (2) can be divided into four steps according to reported literature [20] as follows.

The first step involves hydrolysis of ACh:
(3)
CH 3
COO
(
CH 2
)
2
N +
(
CH 3
)
3
+
H 2
O →
CH 3
COOH + HO
(
CH 2
)
2
N +
(
CH 3
)
3

followed by oxidation of the alcoholic groups:
(4)
NiOOH + HO
(
CH 2
)
2
N +
(
CH 3
)
3
+
OH −
→
OHCCH 2
N +
(
CH 3
)
3
+
H 2
O + Ni
(
OH
)
2
(5)
NiOOH +
HOCCH 2
N +
(
CH 3
)
3
→ ⋅
OCCH 2
N +
(
CH 3
)
3
+ Ni
(
OH
)
2
(6)
NiOOH + ⋅
OCCH 2
N +
CH 3
3
+
OH −
→ -
O O C C
H 2
N +
CH 3
3
+ Ni
OH
2
In the electrocatalytic oxidation process, Ni(OH)2 is oxidized to NiOOH, and then partial NiOOH is reduced to Ni(OH)2 by oxidizing ACh.

For NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE (Fig. 5B), upon adding ACh, the oxidation peak current increased and the reduction peak current decreased, which were similar to those at NiAl-LDH/GCE. However, the increment of oxidation peak current was six times of the decrement of reduction peak current. This interesting phenomenon can be explained as follows. The CDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which bring the negative surface charge, can attract positively charged ACh (present as acetylcholine chloride), and then increases the concentration of ACh on the surface of the modified electrode. Besides, the CDs can also improve the electroconductivity of the modified electrode as described above. So reactions (1) and (2) take place fast and repeatedly in one oxidation process. Therefore, CDs decorating on NiAl-LDHs can enhance the electrocatalytic activity for ACh oxidation.

Amperometry was then used to develop a non-enzymatic biosensor for the determination of ACh. Current-time plots of the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE during the oxidation of ACh were studied in 0.1M KOH solution at +0.65V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Fig. 6 shows the results for the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE under optimized conditions for successive additions of different concentrations of ACh. The inset in Fig. 6A shows a magnified view of the amperometric response for early additions of ACh. Fig. 6B shows a linear relationship between the catalytic current and ACh concentration. The NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE exhibited a linear response from 5 to 6885μM. The linear regression equation is I (μA)=0.009410C (μM)+2.687, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9999. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 1.7μM for ACh at a signal/noise ratio of 3, and the sensitivity is 133.20±0.03mAM−1 cm−2.

The normal ACh concentration range in mammalian nervous systems is 20–60μM [9,33], which is well within the linear response range of the prepared biosensor. Therefore, the prepared biosensor can be used for ACh detection in mammalian nervous systems. However, ACh measurements are comparatively complicated, and some interfering neurochemicals, mainly dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA) and norepinephrine (NE) are co-existing in the mammalian nervous systems [9,33]. The normal physiological concertrations of ACh, DA, AA and NE are 20–60μM, 20μM, 250μM and 2μM, respectively. Therefore, a total of 50μM of ACh, 20μM of DA, 250μM of AA, 2μM of NE and 50μM of ACh were injected into the testing solution of both NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE for selectivity test. The comparation of NiAl-LDH/GCE and NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE for the selectivity is shown in Fig. 7 . It can be seen that the NiAl-LDH/GCE nearly has no selectivity for the four neurochemicals. The interference rates of DA, AA and NE are 61%, 57% and 18%, respectively. But for NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE, the interference rates of DA, AA and NE reduce to 12%, 9.7% and 4.3%, respectively. The increased selectivity can be attributed to that the CDs with negative surface charge can attract the ACh with positive charge, but other interferences like DA, AA and NE, which have negative charge in the alkaline solution will be rejected.

Consecutive CVs were recorded for the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE in 0.1M KOH solution, to verify the durability and stability of the NiAl-LDH/CD composite. The oxidation peak current changed slightly (<2.1%) over 100 cyclic scans. To verify the fabrication reproducibility of the biosensor, five replicate NiAl-LDH/CD/GCEs were prepared under the same conditions. These five yielded a relative standard deviation of 4.7% for the determination of ACh. The long-term stability of the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE was investigated by measuring the electrode response to 2mM ACh. The modified electrode retained 95% of its initial response after storage in a refrigerator at 4°C for 8 weeks.

Table 1 compares the detection potential, linear range, sensitivity, LOD, durability and long-term stability of NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE with those of other reported non-enzymatic ACh electrochemical biosensors [2,26,34,35]. Firstly, it can be seen that the NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE retains a wide linear range (5–6885μM), which is suitable for the ACh detection in mammalian nervous systems with the normal concentration of ACh ranges from 20 to 60μM. Secondly, the prepared biosensor exhibits a high sensitivity (133.20±0.03mAM−1 cm−2) and low LOD (1.7μM), which are superior among the non-enzymatic ACh electrochemical biosensors and are beneficial to collect the measurement signal. Thirdly, the prepared biosensor displays a good durability and long-term stability comparing with other biosensors, which is in favour of the long-term application and also can reduce the cost. These good performances of the prepared biosensor can be attributed to the synergistic effect between NiAl-LDHs and CDs. On the one hand, compared with monodisperse nanoflakes, the cross-linking nanosheet structure and high surface area-to-volume ratio of flower-liked NiAl-LDHs effectively increases the electrode–electrolyte contact area leading to fast reaction kinetics. On the other hand, the negatively surface charged CDs decorating on positively charged brucite-like layers of NiAl-LDHs, improves the electroconductivity and biocompatibility of NiAl-LDHs, and also improves the affinity to ACh.

A non-enzymatic ACh electrochemical biosensor was constructed based on NiAl-LDH/CD composites. Compared with pristine NiAl-LDHs, the NiAl-LDH/CD composites exhibited enhanced electroconductivity and excellent electrocatalytic performance in the oxidation of ACh. The NiAl-LDH/CD/GCE exhibited a wide linear response range, high sensitivity, low LOD, good durability and long-term stability, and excellent selectivity for ACh detection. The NiAl-LDH/CD composites have been proved to be a promising material for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors, and may find wide potential applications in biomedicine, sensor, optronics and catalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the National Basic Research Program of China (Grant No. 2014CB932103), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51272020, 21236003, U1407118), the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (Grant No. 20120010110013), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. YS1406).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amperometric detection of single vesicle acetylcholine release events from an artificial cell
ACS Chem. Neurosci.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lve Wang received her B.Sc. degree in Bioengineering from the Hebei University of Science and Technology, Hebei, PR China, in 2011. She is currently a Ph.D. student under the supervision of Prof. W. S. Yang at the Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, PR China. Her research interests are in electrochemical biosensors based on carbon dots.

Xu Chen received a B.Sc. degree in 1996 and M.Sc. degree in 1999 from Jilin University, and her Ph.D. from the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, PR China, in 2003. She was a postdoctoral research fellow in the Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, from 2003 to 2005. She is presently a professor in the College of Science, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. Her research interests are in nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensors and biosensors.

Changxia Liu graduated from the Department of Biology at the Shandong Normal University in 1991, with the degree of BSc. She was awarded a MSc degree from the Crop Genetic and Breeding Department of the China Agricultural University in 1999, and a Ph.D. (D. E.) from the College of Life Science and Technology, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. Her research interests are in biochemical engineering and the preparation and application of nanomaterials in biotechnology.

Wensheng Yang graduated from the Department of Chemistry at the Shandong University in 1994, with the degree of M.Sc. In 1997, he was awarded a Ph.D. degree from the Department of Physical Chemistry, University of Science and Technology in Beijing. He is presently a professor in the College of Science, Beijing University of Chemical Technology. His research interests are in supramolecular assembly chemistry, new energy storage and conversion materials, and electrochemical sensors.
